text: The surgeon performs a robotic left recurrent laryngeal lymph node dissection for esophageal cancer. The esophagus is visualized in the prone position, with the left and right recurrent laryngeal lymph node packets highlighted in green. The surgeon mobilizes the azygos vein by cutting the parietal pleura above and below it using monopolar scissors. The azygos vein is ligated and cut, then held up by the assistant to expose the prevascular fascia, indicated by a right black arrow. Dissection continues in the prevascular fascia, with the aorta and its arch visible behind.

The surgeon identifies the prevascular fascia in the PIP mode, carefully dissecting along the left border of the esophagus to avoid injury to the thoracic duct. The dissection exposes the ascending and arch of the aorta, revealing the glistening white adventitia of the aorta. The thoracic duct is visible along the aorta. The left recurrent laryngeal nerve is seen hooking underneath the aortic arch. The left vagus nerve is identified and cut in the infra bronchial region.

The surgeon identifies the visceral fascia between the esophagus and trachea, retracting the trachea using cherry forceps, a cherry retractor, and a fan retractor. The dissection proceeds parallel to the left border of the trachea, marked by a white arrow in PIP mode, and follows the visceral pleura along the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet shaded in green. The visceral pleura is cut in an avascular zone while the trachea is retracted towards the surgeon.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to dissect the left recurrent laryngeal lymph node packet, lifting it along with the left recurrent laryngeal nerve and esophagus. The fan retractor is employed to retract the left main bronchus and trachea, exposing the lymph node packet area. The assistant aids by lifting the esophagus and retracting the trachea towards the surgeon.

The surgeon performs dissection within the lymph nodal packet, carefully pushing down the recurrent laryngeal nerve using the robotic right arm with monopolar scissors. The tracheoesophageal groove is cleared of fibro fatty and lymph nodal tissue. The visceral fascia is cut, meeting the prevascular fascia, which is then excised. The thoracic duct is visible in the background.

The surgeon joins the visceral fascia and prevascular fascia, cutting them to expose the aorta beneath and behind the esophagus. The dissection proceeds to the infracoronal part, revealing the right main bronchus and the subcarinal lymph nodal packet. The left main bronchus is fully exposed, and subcarinal dissection is performed, lifting the subcarinal lymph node from the pericardium. The area is cleared of fibro fatty tissue and lymphatic tissue, maintaining a bloodless field by following precise dissection planes.
